critic_raw:
Assessor A: The deployment is threshold-triggered; the evaluation is threshold-independent. This mismatch is the central problem. AUROC 0.88 does not tell nursing leadership how many alerts per shift to expect, what fraction will be actionable, or what the lead time is. All three are needed for clinical governance approval. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed — compute threshold-specific statistics from the retrospective data.

Assessor B: A sepsis alerting system with uncharacterized alarm burden is potentially dangerous, not just suboptimal. Alarm fatigue is a documented cause of adverse patient outcomes. The paper should compute and report the alert rate at candidate thresholds before recommending deployment. The AUROC improvement is encouraging but does not authorize deployment. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

Assessor C: The patient-safety committee member's concern is methodologically correct. Threshold selection is the central clinical decision for an alerting system, not a minor implementation detail. AUROC is a model quality metric; PPV at threshold and alert burden are clinical utility metrics. Both are needed for a deployment decision. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

Synthesis: All three assessors vote empirical_test_agreed. The ETD: compute PPV, alert rate, and lead time at the threshold consistent with nursing capacity constraint from the existing retrospective data.
adjudication_raw: Unanimous empirical_test_agreed. All three identify the same three issues. The ETD is well-specified and the data to perform it exists.

empirical_test.measure: PPV and alert rate per patient-day at the threshold yielding ~2 alerts per 30-bed unit per shift
empirical_test.success_criterion: PPV at this threshold is clinically meaningful and alert rate is within nursing capacity on the retrospective dataset
empirical_test.failure_criterion: PPV at the constrained threshold is below clinical utility or alert rate at acceptable PPV exceeds the 2-per-shift ceiling
